title: Blood on Silk
stainChemistry: Blood proteins (hemoglobin, fibrin, albumin) denature and bond through hydrogen bonding to silk's fibroin protein structure. Heat above 30°C permanently cross-links blood protein to fibers, making removal impossible. Silk IS protein fiber itself—any Protein will digest silk along with blood protein, destroying the garment. Speed and cold water are critical.
whyThisWorks: Ice-cold water halts protein denaturation before hemoglobin oxidizes and sets. NSD surfactant encapsulates protein fragments without alkalinity. Reducing bleach (sodium hydrosulfite, brief) reverses hemoglobin oxidation if needed. This sequence removes fresh blood while preserving silk's protein structure entirely. NO enzymes, ever.

spottingProtocol.equipment: White cotton terry towel, spray bottle
spottingProtocol.instruction: Flush cold water from back of fabric to push blood out before it sets. Flip to front and blot. Never rub. Work from stain edge inward.
Sponge NSD solution onto stain. Blot repeatedly. Work gently — silk fibers are fragile when wet. Check progress and repeat if needed.
Mix equal parts glycerin and cold water. Apply to stain, work gently with soft brush. Glycerin softens remaining protein residue without heat. Blot and check.
Apply acetic acid solution in small drops to remaining stain. Work briefly, then blot with white cloth. Useful for oxidized (older) blood stains.
Flush with NSD to remove all chemistry residue. Work from back of fabric. Blot — do not rub.
Flush with cold water until all detergent and acid residue is removed. Work from back to front. Do NOT use hydrogen peroxide on silk — it damages dyes and fiber.

aftercare: Lay flat on clean towel to air dry. Never wring silk. Never use heat (iron, dryer, hairdryer). If any shadow remains after drying, professional treatment is the next step — do not repeat chemistry.

homeSolutions.instruction: Test on hidden area first — silk is delicate and may not tolerate strong solutions.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Do not use ammonia on silk — it can damage silk dyes and fibers.

homeSolutions.instruction: Place fabric stain-side down on glass work surface. Scrape off any dried blood with a spoon, working gently from center outward.
Dab the stain lightly with cool water applied to a white cloth, lifting blood from the fibers.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Never use hot water — heat sets protein stains on any fiber.

homeSolutions.instruction: Apply mild detergent solution (1/4 tbsp mild laundry detergent + 2 oz cool water) to the stain. Rub extremely gently with a soft cloth, not a spoon.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: Avoid aggressive rubbing on silk — friction damages the fiber.

homeSolutions.instruction: Rinse thoroughly with cool water, working gently from center outward.

If stain remains after step 5, do not apply hydrogen peroxide or any oxidizing agent to silk. Call a professional dry cleaner. Oxidizing agents on silk cause irreversible bleaching of dyes and fiber damage.
homeSolutions.safetyNote: NEVER use hydrogen peroxide on silk — it damages dyes and protein fiber structure. Escalate to professional.

materialWarnings: NEVER Protein enzymes—they digest silk protein fiber as well as blood protein. This destroys the garment.
NEVER hot water (above 30°C)—sets protein through cross-linking permanently.
NEVER chlorine bleach—destroys silk fiber and causes yellowing.
NEVER strong alkaline detergents (pH > 8)—weakens silk's tensile strength.
NEVER mechanical agitation—silk fibers rupture under pressure when wet.
NEVER exceed 60 seconds with reducing bleach on silk—fiber damage risk.
escalation: If stain persists after step 6 or if hemoglobin has oxidized to dark brown, professional dry cleaner is recommended. They have enzyme inhibitor formulations that break hemoglobin-silk bonds safely and can use precise reducing bleach systems without fiber damage. Do not attempt home chemistry on aged blood stains on silk—the oxidation state requires professional expertise.

household.name: White Vinegar
household.note: Acidic spotter for protein residue. Dilute 1:1 with cold water. Safe for silk. DO NOT substitute industrial-strength (28%) acetic acid — it causes chemical burns.

household.name: Mild Liquid Dish Soap (dye-free, fragrance-free)
household.note: Use 1/4 tsp in 1 cup cold water. Gentle pH-neutral substitute for professional NSD. Gentle neutral-pH detergent formulated for silk. 10% solution in cold water.

household.name: Vegetable Glycerin
household.note: Softens protein residue without heat. Mix 1:1 with cold water for soak.

professional.name: Mulsolite
professional.note: Professional-grade NSD. Superior to household detergents for silk protein stains.

professional.name: Sodium Hydrosulfite (Reducing Bleach)
professional.note: For oxidized hemoglobin reversal. Professional-only—precise concentration and timing critical.

customerExplanation: Fresh blood contains proteins that bond to silk through hydrogen attraction. Cold water prevents this bonding from becoming permanent. Our treatment used gentle detergent to encapsulate protein and mild acid to break bonds. If blood oxidized (turned dark brown), professional use of reducing bleach can reverse the oxidation. We never used enzymes—silk IS protein, and enzymes would destroy it.
commonMistakes: Using hot water—this is the #1 mistake. Hot water sets blood protein irreversibly. Once set, home chemistry fails.
Using Protein—people assume enzymes help all protein stains. Protein digests silk's protein structure along with blood, destroying the garment permanently.
Rubbing or scrubbing—silk fibers rupture under pressure when wet, creating feathering and weakness.
Not rinsing reducing bleach thoroughly—residue causes yellowing or gradual fiber weakening.
scienceNote: Hemoglobin's iron center oxidizes from ferrous (Fe²⁺, red) to ferric (Fe³⁺, brown) over hours. Ferric iron bonds more strongly to protein and cellulose fibers through coordinate bonding. This is why fresh blood is easier to remove than old blood—you're removing protein, not fighting iron chemistry. Once ferric, sodium hydrosulfite (reducing agent) converts Fe³⁺ back to Fe²⁺, which releases from fiber more easily. This chemistry works on silk without damaging it, provided dwell time is brief (≤60 sec).
callPro: If stain is older than 4-6 hours or has oxidized dark red/brown, call a professional cleaner who specializes in delicate fabrics. They have access to reducing bleach systems calibrated for silk, enzyme inhibitor formulations, and the expertise to reverse oxidation without fiber damage.
solventNote: Professional dry cleaning: Fresh blood on silk treated with NSD followed by mild acetic acid (GAA diluted 1:10). For oxidized hemoglobin residue, sodium hydrosulfite (reducing bleach) in dilute solution (0.5-1% hydrosulfite) is effective—60 seconds MAXIMUM dwell on silk. VDS post-treatment rinse prevents water spotting. Never exceed recommended reducing bleach concentration or timing on silk.
